sentences.text: That was [INAUDIBLE] 16, the name of the street where that little synagogue is.
And well, like most of us, we're not used to it, to sleep on a bench.
And after two weeks-- it took about four weeks till the Germans occupied Warsaw too-- I said, I want to go home.
So I took my bike and started on my way home.
Like, I didn't know what is a war.
On the way, it was about Zyrardow, from Warsaw, I don't remember how many kilometers.
And me and another man went back to Tomaszow.
And there, they stayed some soldiers.
And I said to him, look at, there are French soldiers.
He said, no, they are Germans.
He was an older man.
And I said, OK, let's go back.
I didn't realize.
And right away, one said halt, [GERMAN].
First what they did, took away my bicycle.
And then they put us on a field, and we did stay there.
Then they start to separate who is Jewish and who is not Jewish.
And one Polish boy, he came over to me, to give him the shoes what I did to wear.
And I said to him, oh, no, you can't get the shoes from me.
And he, I remember, went over to a German and said, he is Jude.
In the meantime, I did hide between other people, because there were a lot of people there.
And I didn't give him the shoes.
Later on, they started to march, that we should march.
And I see that there are coming bullets from all over on the field.
And I did run, me and some-- two other boys, too.
We did run.
And we did wind up in a yard, where there was a little veranda.
And we did stay there overnight.
And then we did proceed to go to Tomaszow by foot.
Yeah, shooting when we did march.
Then we started to escape.
I don't know.
I don't-- apparently they-- a lot of people they took later on to Germany from there.
I don't know what had happened.
But as far as I know, that's what I heard.
Oh, a lot of them, a lot of them.
They did come from all over.
The bullets that did fly, just--
No, I just ran.
I have no idea.
I didn't know in that time what is SS, what is gestapo, and what is plain soldiers.
Yeah, they were the green, those green uniforms.
This I remember.
Oh, quite a lot.
Not only Jews.
A lot of-- oh, I would say in my estimate, was for sure about 1,500 or more.
I don't know.
Because I run.
When I arrived to Tomaszow, I went on [INAUDIBLE] five, where my parents.
I didn't go to my brother.
And then my brother got to know that I am back.
Nojech, the oldest brother.
But he used to live on [INAUDIBLE] eight.
And it happens, where he used to live, he had a very beautiful apartment.
And in one of these rooms from the apartment, the Germans took away.
And they made in that time-- it's called a [GERMAN] station.
They installed something, like telephones or whatever it is.
And right away in that time, some journalists did see a sign, a tailoring.
And right away they came over to my brother, to make for his wive and-- so he got the ride, and then he did hear that I'm back.
So he said to one of the-- I don't know what he was, a captain or whatever it is.
And he said, my brother is here.
He have to be picked up, because he was afraid to walk.
So one German came over with my sister-in-law.
But they didn't walk together.
She did walk apart, about 10 feet or more.
And he did walk.
And then they took me back to his place on [INAUDIBLE] eight.
But still we didn't walk together.
I walked with my sister-in-law and that German.
That's so nobody can say something or arrest me or whatever it is.
Happens nobody-- I wasn't bothered from nobody.
There was no ghetto in that time.
Because in that time, they started-- the first day when they went in to Tomaszow, they arrested a lot of young people and sent them to Buchenwald at that time.
I wasn't there.
That was right when they went into Tomaszow.
When I came back from Warsaw one day, it was done already.
They told me they took away a cousin of me.
And they took away some other people as a matter of fact.
I'm going now.
And I will see-- I always see them, what they survived from Buchenwald.
Nissen Mel, Nissen Mel.
Second name.
He didn't survive.
Oh yeah, plenty.
The one thing that did come to my sister-in-law's brother.
They looked for him, for no reason at all.
Or he did see the Germans, and he did run away.
So later on, they did come around, where is he.
So he wasn't home.
And if somebody runs away after them, they remember, and they did look after him.
And one time they came, and they caught him.
And they used to live in that time already in our apartment-- not in our, in the house.
And they took him out.
And there were steps to go out on the first floor.
And one-- his name was Pichler.
He gave him a bullet right here.
And he rolled over from the steps and fell down.
I didn't see it.
But this is their tactic.
This is what they did.
And I remember his mother did hear the shot.
And she run down.
And she started to scream.
His name was Velvu.
She said, Velvu, what happened?
That German, Pichler, said, what happened, he got crazy.
He fell down from the steps, just like this.
By whom were you told these events?
From who did you get the information about these events?
The mother!
Not my mother, the mother from that--
No.
It couldn't be business.
Because they confiscated it right away.
And wherever it did some business, just-- so that nobody knows.
If one did with the other-- We did work, the tailoring.
Because right away we had some from Germany.
They did come right away with their wives.
And we had some work to do.
And we got a special permit that we can walk.
From a German.
There was a name, the head of the SS.
His name was Grazer.
He was six foot four.
And his wife, I remember, was small.
And we worked for them, and that kept us alive too.
No, me, my father, and my brother.
Everybody did the best what is possible.
If you could do favors for somebody, you did.
Like my brother-in-law-- the oldest, oldest sister-- he did always outside-- he, how do you call this-- with wool, wool.
He always used together and sell it, that he had outside the ghetto.
And through that Grazer, my brother did see that he got a green band, beside that Mogen Dovid, a green band, that he could go out from the ghetto and deal and put together and sell it.
And not sell it-- the main thing he went out, he always got something to eat.
And he could bring in something.
Yeah, we had the white with the David star on-- a white and blue star.
Not to wear?
First of all, they put it on signs all over.
And the second, through the Jewish-- Gemeinde, like Congress, not exactly.
You know, it was called Jewish Gemeinde.
Like there was a president and this.
But they wear the same as we did.
They just had a little-- they were some that were bad, some were very good too, even the Jewish people.
The Jewish council, yeah.
But again, they couldn't be good for everybody.
Well, the result, if they would recognize you are not wearing, they could shoot you, as plain as this.
You could only walk-- if I remember correctly, I think it was till about 6 o'clock.
And it was restricted where to walk.
If you did walk and they did see you, there is no-- they could kill you right away, on the spot.
If-- you didn't know.
Some time you passed by a soldier or whatever they were, and you always wanted to be nice or whatever.
You say good morning or whatever it is.
Somebody could say-- could knock out your teeth and say, what, I am your friend?
Why are you saying good morning to me?
That's what's they-- and some, they answer.
And what would you do, when you walked by a German?
I did try not to walk.
If I did see a German there on this side, I walked on the other side.
Everybody did, not only me.
Yes, one time was-- I was just lucky-- when from the ghetto, they took us to work.
We worked for the Germans.
And we had to go not on the sidewalk, on the road, with the Jewish kapo.
You heard about the kapos.
Like the Jewish police.
And they did this a few times.
All of a sudden, you see that he did call-- he was called Pichler, and one was Fox.
And one did stay here-- we walked like this-- and one did stay here, and one did stay here.
And they walked by.
And they want to see what you have it on.
You might smuggle in something to the shop.
You might smuggle in something to the shop and sell it or whatever it is, in order to get some bread.
So in that time, if they catch somebody with fur, you were killed immediately.
And I remember I had on me some fur, in order to take to the shop and sell it there.
And somehow, some way, they didn't notice.
If they would have noticed whatever it is, I would be killed on the spot.
I think it was in 1942 or '41.
I think between '41 and '42 was established.
Yeah, for sure, in '41.
Yes.
Except my brother.
He went to Russia.
That was my younger brother.
His name, Chaskl
No, we didn't hear any discussion.
As a matter of fact, a week before, I went there to Russia, to Bialystock.
That was occupied by the Russians.
And then from Bialystok, I went to Baranovich.
And I didn't have where to sleep.
To eat, I had.
And I said no, that's not for me.
I'm going back.
And on my way back home, he went at that time to Bialystok.
But we didn't meet each other.
And I did stay in Tomaszow, and he went to Bialystok.
And then he went to Baranovich.
And he got arrested.
He wanted to go to Vilna, in order to come to the States.
Apparently, you could go from Vilna to the states.
And then they send him to Russia, I don't know, to Siberia or what.
And there he passed away.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
Well, that was, that was it.
You have to.
There was put up posters.
And automatically, the Jewish people they start to be together in one place.
That was almost a ghetto.
That was-- some people that did live outside, and then they had to move in.
No, except my brother's place was outside the ghetto.
And then we moved in.
My older brother, Nojech.
We were outside the ghetto until the last day.
Until the last--
Yeah.
I don't recall.
The only thing I recall, we have to move in.
That's all
Yes.
You were registered by the Jewish-- no, I recall arbeit, arbeit-- working.
We were all registered.
[INAUDIBLE], that is right.
The Jewish council.
That they have your-- you're present, whatever.
It's never-- they need you, and they have you.
Yes.
Yeah, there were walls constructed.
No, they were constructed.
Mostly Ukrainians, from--
You mean to go in?
One gate was on [INAUDIBLE], that I know.
I don't remember, there were some other gates.
From there, you can go in.
But you have to go with the Jewish police or some other.
Yes.
Latvian and Ukrainians.
Yeah, yeah.
I don't know.
Whatever they named them, I don't know what they--
Yeah, a lot in black uniforms.
I don't think so.
Maybe there were some Germans, what they were supervising.
But mostly I know they were from Litvia, Latvia and Ukraine.
That's what I know.
Not all the time.
Not all the time, maybe there were some.
I don't recall this.
No.
I had to go with a policeman, if I have to go.
No, no.
The day was always-- when you went out, you didn't know if you were coming back.
Yeah, in the ghetto.
Because you did see a lot of Germans, like in uniform-- SS.
They would come in.
Like, you went down the street.
And whatever said next, next thing you could see, you are dead.
That was not uncommon.
Everybody, I'm sure, did witness this.
I witnessed one time, the way they did-- that was unbelievable what they did to one.
His name was Shama, I remember.
And we did look in through a fence.
That was in the ghetto.
I don't know, they found something in his house or whatever it is.
And they took him down.
And they put on a pail, a pail on his head.
And they clubbed him to death.
And he did beg them, shoot me.
And they said, what do you think?
A bullet doesn't cost money?
Everybody did hear this.
That was in that time Pichler.
He was the main [INAUDIBLE].
They were Germans.
Fox, he was a German Polack.
Finally, they gave him a bullet.
At the end.
You've mentioned Pichler several times.
Pichler?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I know they called him Meister Pichler.
